cards.front: What is a pseudo-random number generator (PRNG)?
cards.back: An algorithm that generates a sequence of numbers that approximates randomness. The numbers are deterministic but appear random.

cards.front: Why do PRNGs need a seed value?
cards.back: The seed is the initial state that determines the sequence of random numbers. The same seed produces the same sequence every time.

cards.text: The C function {{c1::rand()}} should be avoided because it has poor randomness quality and is implementation-dependent.
Modern C++ random number generation facilities are in the {{c1::<random>}} header.

cards.front: What are the two main components needed for random number generation in modern C++?
cards.back:
1) A random number engine (PRNG) that generates random bits
2) A distribution that converts those bits into numbers in a desired range

cards.text: The most commonly used random number engine in C++ is {{c1::std::mt19937}}, which implements the Mersenne Twister algorithm.
To generate random integers in a range, use {{c1::std::uniform_int_distribution<int>}}.

Basic random number generation:
{{c1::std::mt19937 mt;}} // engine
{{c2::std::uniform_int_distribution die{1, 6};}} // distribution
{{c3::int roll = die(mt);}} // generate number

cards.front: What is a common source for seeding a PRNG to get different results each run?
cards.back: std::random_device or the current time. std::random_device is preferred as it attempts to get truly random values from the OS.

cards.text:
To seed a Mersenne Twister engine with random_device:
{{c1::std::random_device rd;}} // obtain random seed
{{c2::std::mt19937 mt{rd()};}} // seed the engine

cards.front: Should you create a new random engine every time you need a random number?
cards.back: No, create the engine once (with seed) and reuse it. Creating it repeatedly, especially with the same seed, defeats randomness.

cards.front: What distribution would you use for random floating-point numbers?
cards.back: std::uniform_real_distribution<double> for uniformly distributed real numbers in a range.

cards.concept: Modern C++ random number generation pattern
cards.explanation:
The proper pattern for random numbers:
  • Create engine once, seed it with std::random_device
  • Make it static or global so it persists
  • Create distribution for desired range
  • Call distribution with engine: dist(engine)
  • Reuse same engine for multiple numbers
  • Never use rand() - use <random> facilities
examples:
// Good: reusable setup
static std::mt19937 mt{std::random_device{}()};
std::uniform_int_distribution die{1, 6};
int roll = die(mt); // generate

// Bad: recreating each time
std::mt19937 mt{1}; // same seed every time!

cards.front: Why should you avoid using rand() and srand() from C?
cards.back: They produce poor quality randomness, are implementation-dependent, have limited range, and modern C++ provides far better alternatives in <random>.
